item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author stated they wanted the term to stay amnesiac but someone changed it

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As the journal is licensed under All Rights Reserved, the image is non-compliant with the SCP Wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One question: Is it sensible to recommend a specific antibiotic for all cases, our should it have been a type of antibiotics that was recommended?

Corollary: If it is not sensible, is it true to life tgat someone would still recommend a specific antibiotic?

—-

OK, what is the second thing this skip is linked to? I will never figure it out on my own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A solid biological horror scip. While the interview is a bit choppy in regards to grammar and sentence structure it makes perfect sense to me as it is translated from Hungarian. People often forget how different the structure of different languages are. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tin foil hats are part of the containment procedures… And this is scary.
Instant +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And with my upvote this has reached +100. Congrats VAElynx on a great piece of work. This one made my skin crawl and that isn't a easy task.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I recall right, a fluid-filled sack with cillia on the inside, used for both vibration pickup and orientation (fluid with just a bit of a bubble, similar to a water level).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is happening to many Skips, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something weird is going on at the top of the page. Rating box is in the wrong spot and brackets appear above and below it for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot for the life of me tell what that "sensory organ" iin the picture is supposed to be and it's aggravating me not knowing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It either grows legs and does the Birdwalk or it just uses the corners to hop around

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I ever have to encounter this bugger I'm wearing tin-foil everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I mean is that it doesn't sound correct in English, re the interjections. On purpose

I'm not sure myself. It works in my head, but then, I think in my mind the dialogue developed in slovak, so it might be a leftover of that process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I mean is that it doesn't sound correct in English, re the interjections. On purpose?

As for the rest, I think you're being silly, but it's a fair assessment of how silly staff policy is with regards that issue (and I think a mass correction would be totally valid since we did the same damn thing with metric), so… fair's fair. I'm not going to downvote an article this good over it regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't make sense; is that deliberate? I'd change 'thank' to 'thanks', but I'm not sure what was intended here.

It's… interjections. Verbal punctuation. He was.. well, a mix of emotions, between being thankful for it not being able to remove his belt, and having the mental image of just what it would have done if it had.

EDIT: By the way, did you use "amnesiacs" on purpose, or might you mind changing that to "amnestics"?

I always did and will. I started writing before it became an obsolete term, if you will, and I guess it's… my way of protesting the idiotic twin standard. Because retaining site(OOC)-wide use of "amnesiac" would be far less idiotic than having both in use, one obviously correct and one not. So I just sort of pretend "amnestic" never existed, because despite the obvious will to do much more substantial edits without author approval (see chopping off of Keter duty addenda, which irks me to this day), a simple terminology fix is apparently way beyond the limit of what is in the staff powers to do (and other assorted excuses for something untenable).

Fun fact - as of a year and a half ago, I catalogued all of the uses of "amnesiac" on the site, and there was precisely one instance of it being used correctly in an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is still really good. I fixed a few typos, mostly ".." instead of "…".

Didn't make with the belt, fuck - thank to Virgin Mary - hell.

This doesn't make sense; is that deliberate? I'd change 'thank' to 'thanks', but I'm not sure what was intended here.

EDIT: By the way, did you use "amnesiacs" on purpose, or might you mind changing that to "amnestics"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the scip, but the interview is just… weird in parts. I think it's just the style of the guy being interviewed, but I get that he wasn't supposed to sound all clinical or super smart like a researcher, I just don't like it.

Still give it a +1. Overall a good entry.

item.title: Image attribution
item.content:encoded:
Image comes from a medical journal entitled: "Giant Mature Cystic Teratoma of the Adrenal Region"

Citation:
American Journal of Roentgenology. 2004;183: 837-838. 10.2214/ajr.183.3.1830837

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One point is a bit unclear: The description says SCP-1308 is 'ambulatory' which means that it walks. Everywhere else in the article it appears to be flying. So I'm left unclear on: (a) when does it fly and when does it walk? Under what circumstances? And: (b) what does it actually mean to say a sheet of skin walks? What does that look like?

It would be great if that could be clarified a little bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview log is pretty much perfect. Addresses the major problem I have with most such logs: unbelievable dialogue.

item.title: Good writing
item.content:encoded: Good, solid writing. I really liked the work here. And I want to be the nerd that says "I once killed a bunch of this with my party. The cleric had a field day healing us all".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.
Also, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how this works; there was just one thing that made me do a double take. The victim's first name, aside from the initial, is blackboxed in the recovery log, but not in the section with the interview. I think it'd be best to stick with one for both, whether that means blackboxing or not, just for consistency's sake, but it's not that big of a problem.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Impressive! This works, and it works well!

There are still a few errors splattered here and there; I'll do a quick fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. Though the article is very long, and I started skimming a bit about half-way through the description, the detail contained within is very impressive and I respect the amount of research is must have taken to write that. The interview-log put the statistics we had been given previously into perspective and added a nice punch of horror.

I particularly like that this particular SCP isn't even lethal most of the time. A brilliant example of how something doesn't need to be fatal to be terrifying.

The picture, of course, was PERFECT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hee, glad you liked it.
I had some fun thinking up that particular bit of dialogue. The purpose of it was mainly to foster cooperation - it made him less likely to freak out or think too much about what he seen instead of reporting it like he remembers , when he was persuaded it was a mundane incident.
Plus, administration of amnesiacs became easier :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher F█████: Your case isn't the first, Mr. B█████. What you encountered appears to be an invasive species of constrictor snake, accidentally imported from Uzbekistan. We just need to confirm some details before it can be properly reported and dealt with.

J.B.: Snake? It looked nothing like a fucking snake… It was… I dunno… Like a skin… Live skin, like on my arm. Hell, it even had hair. And it was flying.

Researcher F█████: It can float for short distances. It's a relatively rare species, it just has better living conditions under our geography. A lot like rabbits in Australia. But regardless. Your colleague told us he found you lying in the maintenance hall. Can you tell me what happened?

This was what really got me…. a realistic look at how the foundation builds cover stories on cover stories, considering this guy was already going to be amnesiaced, but they still took the time to presumably improv another cover story about flying snakes from Uzbekistan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one who got confused by "flotation" and thought these were aquatic at first?
No? Just me? Okay then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy cheese what is that a picture of.

Upvote :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find it hysterical that someone (I'm assuming their initials were RR) gives permission to rewrite an entry, seemingly under the assumption that it would just fail again, and then gets pissy when it's done well… Because this is obviously an ultra elitist in-crowd that hasn't consistently praised newcomers that do well, and encouraged those that don't to do better. Unless they're incredibly terribad.

Also, Vae, you have made me thoroughly uncomfortable in my own skin, moreso than the unseasonable heat and humidty. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which is why mine was aluminum foil instead. :P Based on my limited research, aluminum passivates much more easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better execution than the prior attempt. It made me repulsed, rather than intrigued, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah. Thanks for that - Quite some time ago, you told me of that challenge, and I knew you had a procedure like that in your sandbox, just unused. I was thinking of stuff to put into this one… let's say that it was the second kick needed to sort this SCP out.

EDIT: Though, truth be told, tin doesn't passivate easily, unless exposed to SCP-1308 's secretions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed the tin-foil hat thing too. Very similar to the quickie passivation one I wrote up in my sandbox, only much better. Congratulations. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I think I would also be wearing tin foil underwear around this thing too.

An actual intelligent use for a tin foil hat as well as everything else means a definite +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And that's where I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. *clicks plus*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Considering that he deleted his account without removing his downvote first? I'd say go ahead, unless someone else has already said otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that was a monumental waste of some very good potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I had good ideas, one of them would have worked. None of them did. I'm deleting my account, and wiping my hands of this place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even Boa Noah agrees. You have good ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You fail to understand what people are saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have any potential. All my ideas tank, and I've had enough. I'm not coming here anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When it's better written, it gets upvotes. What do you think happens when veteran writers take good ideas (which you had) and applies good execution (which you lacked)? This is exactly what we told you could happen if you did some serious rewriting. Can you not see what this article has that yours didn't?

If anything, this should demonstrate your potential. Take advantage of the compliment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … you have got to be kidding me. :/

item.title: Oh man...
item.content:encoded: This SCP kicks ass.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't follow the other thread before it got deleted, but people aren't having an issue with the base ideas you're throwing out there. It's the execution of the writing that they are complaining about.

This submission is, frankly, a much better article than yours was.

I don't know if you're like me and just don't want to get onto IRC, but even the best writers in the world collaborate with an editor and bounce drafts off of other people before publishing.

Find someone you can collaborate with on submissions before throwing them onto the site, and you'll probably get a much better reception.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
uggggghhh this one made me a bit queasy. Skin stuff is.. unsettling to me :L
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did a rewrite of the article, with his explicit permission. Sorts and Photo can confirm.
If you are interested, the original is still stored in my sandbox as a sample, along with this.

EDIT: Actually given the content of the above post, I'm claiming malicious downvoting, and requesting being allowed to self-upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*puts Mod Pants on*
What seems to be the problem here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You can't be serious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course. When it's submitted by someone else, everyone loves it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Note: Aluminium foil is not an acceptable substitute for tin in this case.

See, tin foil hats are useful in some circumstances!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this it's good but it could be tighter, upvoted tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *sigh* Consider doing favours to someone else next time, plz =^.^;= .. even just now , I had to fix a few errors you introduced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as an aside.
This sort of succeeded at the ancient Yoric's challenge - write an object whose containment procedures involve wearing a tin foil hat.

Also, I wonder if someone catches on the possible connection between this one ,and … something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hee. Actually it's a teratoma.
Doesn't look entirely like what I wanted, but a good enough choice of label made it work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very much improved, with an excellent choice of picture-degloved skin? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. OK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: upvoting, I'll do you a favor and go over the grammar/vocabulary to fix it up, make it a it more readable. I won't do anything other than that, I swear.
